claims.text: Horror media is a multibillion-dollar industry with Halloween alone accounting for roughly eight billion dollars a year in US spending.

evidence.quote: horror movies are a billion dollar business
Halloween spending is about eight billion a year

claims.rationale: NRF surveys consistently report US Halloween spending in the $8-12B range during the 2020s. Global horror box office plus streaming and gaming easily exceeds $10B/year. Widely-documented figures.

claims.text: Fear is defined by the Oxford English Dictionary as the emotion of pain or uneasiness caused by a sense of impending danger.

evidence.quote:
According to the Oxford English Dictionary, fear is defined as the emotion of pain or
uneasiness caused by a sense of impending danger or by the

claims.rationale: This quotation matches the OED's definition of 'fear' as an emotion aroused by sense of threat. Definitional claim with direct dictionary backing.

claims.text: Humans enjoy scary experiences when they perceive an underlying safety net because the brain recognizes the absence of actual threat.

evidence.quote: It's sort of a way to hack your brain chemistry

claims.rationale: The safety-net or protective-frame theory of recreational fear is well-established in psychology (Apter's reversal theory; Kerr & Apter 1991; Andrade & Cohen 2007 'the coexistence of pleasure and fear'). It aligns with mainstream affective-neuroscience accounts of vicarious-threat enjoyment.

claims.text: During recreational fear, the brain releases adrenaline, endorphins, and dopamine producing a euphoric biochemical rush.

evidence.quote: We experience a rush of adrenaline and a release of endorphins and dopamine
The biochemical rush can result in a sense of euphoria and heightened awareness

claims.rationale: Acute sympathetic activation during fear does release epinephrine/norepinephrine along with endorphins and increased dopaminergic activity in mesolimbic pathways. The combined 'arousal + reward' profile is standard affective neuroscience.

claims.text: Dr. Margee Kerr spent 10 years researching why people enjoy fear.

evidence.quote: Marjie, Kyra, spent 10 years investigating fear

claims.rationale: Margee Kerr (sociologist, University of Pittsburgh, author of 'Scream: Chilling Adventures in the Science of Fear') has genuinely spent over a decade researching recreational fear, including work with the ScareHouse attraction. The video mispronounces her name.

dependencies.rationale: Kerr's research underpins the chemical-release claim.

claims.text: Shared scary experiences with friends or family elevate oxytocin levels and strengthen social bonds.

evidence.quote:
doing frightening things is much more fun when you're with
friends or family
this burst of emotion is due to raise the oxytocin levels

claims.rationale: Oxytocin release during coordinated high-arousal group experiences is supported in social-neuroscience literature (e.g., Dunbar, Uvnas-Moberg), though the mechanism is better established for positive than fear-based bonding. Medium-high directTruth for the general claim, slightly overstated for recreational fear specifically.

claims.text: The amygdala, located behind the prefrontal cortex, governs fear processing and expression.

evidence.quote:
Your amygdala, the part of your brain connected to and just behind the prefrontal cortex
is in charge of what makes you afraid

claims.rationale: The amygdala's central role in fear conditioning, threat detection, and fear-response initiation is canonical neuroscience (LeDoux et al.). Spatial description is slightly imprecise - the amygdala sits deeper in the temporal lobe, not strictly behind the PFC - but the functional claim is correct.

claims.text: Panic disorders and PTSD are associated with underactive prefrontal cortex, while OCD and generalized anxiety feature overactive PFC.

evidence.quote: people with panic disorders and PTSD have an underactive prefrontal cortex

Those with worry based anxiety like OCD and generalized anxiety disorder usually have an
overactive prefrontal cortex

claims.rationale: PTSD literature generally shows reduced ventromedial PFC activity consistent with diminished amygdala regulation. OCD's orbitofrontal-cortex hyperactivity is well established. But the clean 'underactive vs overactive' dichotomy oversimplifies a heterogeneous fMRI literature with mixed findings. Medium directTruth.

claims.text: Fewer dopamine autoreceptors makes adrenaline junkies crave thrilling situations because they get more dopamine release per scare.

evidence.quote: People who enjoy fear may have a different number of auto receptors than others
People with fewer dopamine receptors may get more dopamine from a scare

claims.rationale: The autoreceptor-and-sensation-seeking connection draws on David Zald's 2008 dopamine-autoreceptor PET work showing low-autoreceptor individuals score higher on novelty-seeking. However, the cleaner 'adrenaline junkies = dopamine seekers' framing is a popularization that oversimplifies multi-factorial personality neuroscience. Medium directTruth.

claims.text: Not everyone enjoys recreational fear because life experiences and personality differences shape whether being scared registers as fun or aversive.

evidence.quote: People who prefer rom coms or comedy over horror

claims.rationale: The claim that individual differences (temperament, prior exposure, bad childhood experiences) modulate recreational-fear enjoyment is a common opinion-style summary. Not a strongly testable empirical claim; omitted directTruth because it's framed as a generalization about personality rather than a specific fact.

claims.text: Humans evolved a useful fear response through natural selection: those without it became sabertooth tiger food.

evidence.quote: Marjie, Kyra, spent 10 years investigating fear

claims.rationale: Evolutionary-psychology account of fear as an adaptive threat-response is widely accepted. Sabertooth cats and other predators were real selection pressures during Pleistocene. Classic just-so story framing, but the underlying evolutionary logic (fear reduces predation mortality) is sound.

dependencies.rationale: Evolved amygdala-driven fear response is the mechanism behind the adaptive account.
